message.content: Adversary-in-the-Middle (AiTM) attacks involve adversaries positioning themselves between networked devices to intercept, monitor, or manipulate data. By exploiting networking protocols like ARP, DNS, and LLMNR, attackers can reroute traffic through their systems, enabling activities such as network sniffing, data manipulation, and credential theft. Techniques include DNS manipulation to redirect users or deploy malware, and protocol downgrade attacks to weaken encryption (e.g., SSL/TLS). AiTM can also facilitate network denial of service (DoS) attacks by disrupting traffic flow. Notable examples include Dok, which proxies web traffic to alter HTTP(S) data, and Kimsuky, which uses modified PHProxy for traffic examination. Mitigation strategies include enforcing strong encryption protocols, implementing network segmentation, using secure DNS configurations, and deploying intrusion detection systems to monitor for suspicious traffic patterns.
